contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Classify unfamiliar stack code from retained-state semantics, a maintained order invariant, the exact removal rule, and its correctness argument—not from names, indices, comparisons, loops, or asymptotic cost alone.

authoringProvenance.author: OpenAI GPT-5.6 Pro — candidate authoring assistant; human review required

items.prompt: A design review covers Four ordinary counterexamples. Reject “uses a stack” as sufficient evidence of monotonic-stack semantics. Which statement gives the decisive justification?
constraints: Use the exact output, strictness, scan direction, and tie semantics stated in the prompt.
Do not classify the algorithm from a variable name or one intermediate snapshot.

options.text: Ordinary nesting, history, evaluation, and traversal all use LIFO without ordered candidates.
Every stack problem is monotonic.
One surface feature—such as a variable name, indices, comparisons, repeated pops, or linear time—is sufficient to classify the stack as monotonic.
If the retained values appear sorted at any one point in the trace, the algorithm is monotonic by definition.

feedback.reason: Ordinary nesting, history, evaluation, and traversal all use LIFO without ordered candidates. This is the decisive boundary for the stated output and retained-state semantics.
feedback.details: The item is decided by positive semantic evidence required before calling a stack monotonic, not by a surface feature. Ordinary nesting, history, evaluation, and traversal all use LIFO without ordered candidates. The tempting claim—Every stack problem is monotonic.—fails because it does not establish the semantic invariant that makes removal or retention safe. Classify unfamiliar stack code from retained-state semantics, a maintained order invariant, the exact removal rule, and its correctness argument—not from names, indices, comparisons, loops, or asymptotic cost alone.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.target_false_heuristic: This is the targeted misconception. Ordinary nesting, history, evaluation, and traversal all use LIFO without ordered candidates. The quoted clue does not establish that invariant or justify the removal rule.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.surface_feature_classifier: This relies on a surface feature instead of the contract. The correct decision follows from positive semantic evidence required before calling a stack monotonic; payload type or implementation shape alone is not diagnostic.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.unjustified_alternative_rule: This changes the representation or transition without proving that it preserves the requested result. Ordinary nesting, history, evaluation, and traversal all use LIFO without ordered candidates.

If the retained values appear sorted at any one point in the trace, the algorithm is monotonic by definition.
Names do not establish maintained order or safe deletion.

feedback.reason: Names do not establish maintained order or safe deletion. This is the decisive boundary for the stated output and retained-state semantics.
feedback.details: Start from the output contract and the meaning of each retained entry. Names do not establish maintained order or safe deletion. The misconception “Identifier choice proves algorithm category.” confuses an implementation clue with a correctness condition. items.prompt: A trace shows retained values in sorted order at one instant. Select all reasons this snapshot is insufficient to prove a monotonic stack.
constraints: Select all and only the statements that remain valid under the exact output and tie policy.
Each selected statement must be justified by retained-state semantics or a correctness/cost argument.

options.text: A later legal push can break incidental order when no maintenance rule restores it.
Any sorted snapshot is a complete inductive proof of monotonicity.
The variable name determines whether the observed order is meaningful.
An invariant must hold after every legal transition, not only in one observed state.
The order must be tied to unresolved-candidate meaning and the requested output.

feedback.reason: The accepted set jointly establishes the decisive boundary: Incidental order is not an inductive invariant tied to output correctness.
feedback.details: The stack category follows from the proof obligation. Incidental order is not an inductive invariant tied to output correctness. Names, indices, comparisons, loop shape, or one trace snapshot cannot replace that proof. In this case, the stated misconception—Observed sorted values establish monotonicity.—would permit transitions that are not justified by the requested result. Classify unfamiliar stack code from retained-state semantics, a maintained order invariant, the exact removal rule, and its correctness argument—not from names, indices, comparisons, loops, or asymptotic cost alone.

items.prompt: An implementation must handle Postfix or collision example. Which statement correctly determines the stack discipline or rule required by the contract?
constraints: Use the exact output, strictness, scan direction, and tie semantics stated in the prompt.
Do not classify the algorithm from a variable name or one intermediate snapshot.

options.text: If the retained values appear sorted at any one point in the trace, the algorithm is monotonic by definition.
Evaluation, collision resolution, and local validation may compare values without maintaining a frontier.
Any top/current comparison is monotonic.
One surface feature—such as a variable name, indices, comparisons, repeated pops, or linear time—is sufficient to classify the stack as monotonic.

feedback.reason: Evaluation, collision resolution, and local validation may compare values without maintaining a frontier. This is the decisive boundary for the stated output and retained-state semantics.

Classify unfamiliar stack code from retained-state semantics, a maintained order invariant, the exact removal rule, and its correctness argument—not from names, indices, comparisons, loops, or asymptotic cost alone.

items.prompt: Select all valid reasons that storing indices does not by itself identify a monotonic stack.
constraints: Select all and only the statements that remain valid under the exact output and tie policy.
Each selected statement must be justified by retained-state semantics or a correctness/cost argument.

options.text: A parser may retain token offsets for unmatched structural openers.
An explicit DFS stack may retain node indices as pending traversal work.
A monotonic stack may retain indices for distance or duplicate provenance, but its classification still comes from the ordered candidate invariant.
Indices are used only by monotonic-stack algorithms.
A value-only stack is necessarily ordinary and can never be monotonic.

feedback.reason: The accepted set jointly establishes the decisive boundary: Indices encode provenance in many ordinary and monotonic algorithms.
feedback.details: Start from the output contract and the meaning of each retained entry. Indices encode provenance in many ordinary and monotonic algorithms. That mechanism explains both the legal transition and the point at which an answer or pending state becomes final. The misconception “Indices are a monotonic-stack signature.” confuses an implementation clue with a correctness condition. Classify unfamiliar stack code from retained-state semantics, a maintained order invariant, the exact removal rule, and its correctness argument—not from names, indices, comparisons, loops, or asymptotic cost alone.

items.prompt: Select all parts of a complete positive test for classifying unfamiliar pseudocode as a monotonic-stack algorithm.
constraints: Select all and only the statements that remain valid under the exact output and tie policy.
Each selected statement must be justified by retained-state semantics or a correctness/cost argument.

options.text: The total running time is O(n), so the semantic category is determined.
Retained entries are unresolved candidates ordered by a declared key.
The current item removes candidates through an exact comparison relation.
The removal proof shows that each popped candidate is finalized or can never affect a future valid answer.
The code stores indices and contains a while-loop.

feedback.reason: The accepted set jointly establishes the decisive boundary: Require ordered unresolved candidates, exact comparison-driven removal, and a proof that removal finalizes or preserves all future answers.
